title: `$fatal` leads to coredump and should not be affected by `+verilator+error+limit`
body:
```sv
module m;
  always #1 $display($time);

  final $display("final");

  initial begin
    #3;
    $fatal("Timeout");
  end
endmodule
```
Compilation & running:
```
verilator --binary -j 0 -MAKEFLAGS '--silent' --quiet m.sv && obj_dir/Vm +verilator+quiet
```
Output:
```
                   1
                   2
[3] %Fatal: m.sv:8: Assertion failed in TOP.m
%Error: m.sv:8: Verilog $stop
Aborting...
Aborted (core dumped)
```

v5.024-56-g5f85c8942 5f85c8942 on ArchLinux

Problems:
- According to LRM 20.10 `$fatal` implicitly calls `$finish`, which should execute the `final` block.
- It leads to coredump somehow

Other observations:
- Verilator allows `+verilator+error+limit+`, this also overrides `$fatal`. Is this intended? IMHO `+verilator+error+limit+` is great for continuing the simulation a bit to see the context. However `$fatal` should override error limit.
- Also `$error` does not execute the `final` block. `final` should be executed at the end of the simulation. How would you interpret *the end of simulation*?
